perturbation.original_text: The Adviser hereby agrees to indemnify and save harmless FASC and its trustees, officers and employees (each of the foregoing an "Indemnified Party" and collectively the "Indemnified Parties") against and from any and all losses, obligations, penalties, actions, judgments and suits and other costs, expenses and disbursements of any kind or nature whatsoever which may be imposed on, incurred by or asserted against an Indemnified Party, other than as a consequence of gross negligence or willful misconduct on the part of an Indemnified Party, arising out of or in connection with this Limited Power of Attorney or any other agreement, instrument or document executed in connection with the exercise of the authority granted to FASC herein to act on behalf of the Adviser, including without limitation the reasonable costs, expenses and disbursements in connection with defending such Indemnified Party against any claim or liability related to the exercise or performance of any of FASC's powers or duties under this Limited Power of Attorney or any of the other agreements, instruments or documents executed in connection with the exercise of the authority granted to FASC herein to act on behalf of the Adviser, or the taking of any action under or in connection with any of the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: The Adviser hereby agrees to indemnify and save harmless FASC and its trustees, officers and employees (each of the foregoing an "Indemnified Party" and collectively the "Indemnified Parties") against and from any and all losses, obligations, penalties, actions, judgments and suits and other costs, expenses and disbursements of any kind or nature whatsoever which may be imposed on, incurred by or asserted against an Indemnified Party, other than as a consequence of simple negligence on the part of an Indemnified Party, arising out of or in connection with this Limited Power of Attorney or any other agreement, instrument or document executed in connection with the exercise of the authority granted to FASC herein to act on behalf of the Adviser, including without limitation the reasonable costs, expenses and disbursements in connection with defending such Indemnified Party against any claim or liability related to the exercise or performance of any of FASC's powers or duties under this Limited Power of Attorney or any of the other agreements, instruments or documents executed in connection with the exercise of the authority granted to FASC herein to act on behalf of the Adviser, or the taking of any action under or in connection with any of the foregoing.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the exception from "gross negligence or willful misconduct" to "simple negligence" broadens the indemnity provided to FASC, potentially violating fiduciary duties under the Investment Company Act of 1940.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 17(h) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 restricts exculpation of investment company personnel for conduct falling below certain standards. The modification eliminates the gross negligence and willful misconduct standard, potentially allowing indemnity for actions that violate these fiduciary responsibilities and thereby contradicting the Act.

Transactions of certain affiliated persons and underwriters (a) Prohibited transactions It shall be unlawful for any affiliated person or promoter of or principal underwriter for a registered investment company (other than a company of the character described in section 80a–12(d)(3)(A) and (B) of this title),
perturbation.justification: The change from "gross negligence or willful misconduct" to "simple negligence" significantly broadens the indemnity, potentially contradicting Section 17(h) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 as explained in the 'Law Explanation'. The original text only indemnified FASC for actions not arising from their gross negligence or willful misconduct, aligning with fiduciary responsibilities. By indemnifying against simple negligence, the changed text potentially shields FASC from liability even for breaches of their duty of care, conflicting with the protective intent of the Investment Company Act. Snippet 1 shows the codification of the Investment company act in 15 USC 80a-17, while snippet 2 shows 15 USC 80a-17 and related CFRs.

perturbation.original_text: Any person, partnership, corporation or other legal entity dealing with FASC in its capacity as attorney-in-fact hereunder for the Adviser on behalf of any Fund is hereby expressly put on notice that FASC is acting solely in the capacity as an agent of the Adviser as agent for the Fund and that any such person, partnership, corporation or other legal entity must look solely to the Fund in question for enforcement of any claim against the Fund, as FASC assumes no personal liability whatsoever for obligations of the Fund entered into by FASC in its capacity as attorney-in-fact for the Adviser.
perturbation.changed_text: Any person, partnership, corporation or other legal entity dealing with FASC in its capacity as attorney-in-fact hereunder for the Adviser on behalf of any Fund is hereby expressly put on notice that FASC is acting solely in its capacity as an agent of the Adviser as agent for the Fund and that any such person, partnership, corporation or other legal entity must look solely to the Adviser in question for enforcement of any claim against the Fund, as FASC assumes no personal liability whatsoever for obligations of the Fund entered into by FASC in its capacity as attorney-in-fact for the Adviser.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "Fund" to "Adviser" for claim enforcement contradicts the purpose of investment funds, which are legally distinct entities from the investment advisor.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 1 of the Investment Company Act of 1940 states, in relevant part, that investment companies (funds) are entities with distinct legal existences, separate from their advisors. This modification seeks to make the investment advisor solely responsible for the fund's obligations. Directing claims to the adviser rather than the fund directly violates the structure of the fund and advisor relationship established under the ICA.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 80a-1 - Findings and declaration of policy | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 80a-1 - Findings and declaration of policy U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Findings Upon the basis of facts disclosed by the record and reports of the Securities and Exchange Commission made pursuant to section 79z–4 [1] of this title, and facts otherwise disclosed and ascertained, it is found that investment companies are affected with a national public interest in that, among other things— (1) the securities issued by such companies, which constitute a substantial part of all securities publicly offered, are distributed, purchased, paid for, exchanged, transferred, redeemed, and repurchased by use of the mails and means and instrumentalities of interstate commerce , and in the case of the numerous companies which issue redeemable securities this process of distribution and redemption is continuous; (2) the principal activities of such companies—investing, reinvesting, and trading in securities—are conducted by use of the mails and means and instrumentalities of interstate commerce , including the facilities of national securities exchanges , and constitute a substantial part of all transactions effected in the securities markets of the Nation; (3) such companies customarily invest and trade in securities issued by, and may dominate and control or otherwise affect the policies and management of, companies engaged in business in interstate commerce ; (4) such companies are media for the investment in the national economy of a substantial part of the national savings and may have a vital effect upon the flow of such savings into the capital markets; and (5) the activities of such companies, extending over many States , their use of the instrumentalities of interstate commerce and the wide geographic distribution of their security hol
perturbation.justification: The modification directs claims against the 'Adviser' instead of the 'Fund,' directly contradicting the principle that investment funds are distinct legal entities. The Investment Company Act of 1940, as indicated in the Law Explanation, establishes this separation. While the snippets provided don't explicitly detail the liability of the adviser versus the fund, they point to 15 U.S.C. § 80a-1, which discusses the national public interest in investment companies and their operations. The change undermines this structure by misaligning responsibility for claims, thus contradicting the intended legal framework where funds are responsible for their obligations, not the advisor.

perturbation.original_text: This Limited Power of Attorney shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania without reference to principles of conflicts of laws.
perturbation.changed_text: This Limited Power of Attorney shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the People's Republic of China without reference to principles of conflicts of laws.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Pennsylvania to the People's Republic of China makes the contract unenforceable in US courts due to lack of jurisdiction and conflicts of law principles, especially regarding securities regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Choice of Law Principles in US Contract Law
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a direct statute, choice of law is a well-established legal principle. US courts generally respect choice of law provisions, but not when it violates fundamental principles of the forum state (here, Pennsylvania, where the Adviser is located and presumably operates). Selecting PRC law creates significant enforceability issues as it is unlikely to have a 'substantial relationship' to the agreement. Additionally, enforcing PRC law would contravene securities regulations in the US meant to protect US investors.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from Pennsylvania to the People's Republic of China creates a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation indicates that enforcing PRC law could contravene US securities regulations designed to protect US investors. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines conflict of laws as 'a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute.' Selecting PRC law introduces a conflict of laws issue, making the contract unenforceable in US courts.
